metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either enumerate `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` (line 208 hardcoded var list) as an edit target for S4, or add an explicit prerequisite that S1 must land before S4 to keep `make test` green (the mcp-reference.md drift gate will otherwise fail on the new `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_INTROSPECTION_*` env vars).
findings.finding: `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` is an unenumerated consumer that will break CI when new `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_INTROSPECTION_*` entries are added to `MCP_ENV_VARS`. The 'Gate environment variables' table at line 208 of that script iterates a hardcoded tuple of exactly three var names (`REBAR_MCP_READONLY`, `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM`, `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`), not `MCP_ENV_VARS` itself. The plan's AC7 (`make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass) includes the `mcp-reference.md` drift gate in CI — yet the plan does not enumerate `gen_mcp_reference.py` as a file this story must touch, deferring all generator fixes to S1/S7. If S1 has not landed when S4 ships, the mcp-reference.md drift gate will fail, making AC7 unachievable as written.
findings.location: AC bullet 7 (`make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass) / scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py:208
findings.suggested_fix: Add `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` to the edit set for S4 with the instruction: extend the hardcoded Gate env vars table (line 208) to iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS` dynamically — or, as a minimal patch, append the new vars to the hardcoded tuple. Alternatively, make S1 a hard prerequisite (not just a dependency) and state that S4's AC7 is only verifiable after S1 lands.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the private-host check either (a) resolves the URL hostname to its IP(s) at construction time (e.g. via `socket.getaddrinfo`) before applying the RFC-1918/loopback/169.254 check, or (b) explicitly documents that the check is IP-literal-only and that DNS rebinding is an accepted out-of-scope threat requiring network-level controls.
findings.finding: The private-host rejection check is specified to operate 'at construction' on the endpoint URL, but the plan never says the hostname is resolved to an IP before checking. For non-literal hostnames (e.g. 'introspect.example.com'), the construction-time check only sees the DNS name — a DNS rebinding attack or misconfigured split-horizon DNS can make a public-looking hostname resolve to 127.0.0.1 or an RFC-1918 address, bypassing the guard entirely. The check is sound only for literal IP addresses in the URL.
findings.location: Security section — 'Private/link-local endpoint hosts (RFC 1918, loopback, 169.254.0.0/16) are rejected at construction'; AC5 — 'a private/link-local host is rejected by default'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section, add: 'At construction, resolve the endpoint hostname to its IP address(es) via socket.getaddrinfo and apply the RFC-1918/loopback/link-local check to the resolved IPs, not only to the URL host string. If resolution fails, reject construction (fail-closed). This prevents DNS-based SSRF bypass for non-literal hostnames.'

findings.finding: AC6 specifies an integration test using 'httpx `ASGITransport` wrapping the MCP server', but `build_server()` returns a `FastMCP` (stdio-transport) instance — no ASGI interface is established anywhere in the codebase, and ALL existing integration tests invoke the server by calling `srv.call_tool()` / `srv.list_tools()` directly, never via HTTP/ASGI. If `FastMCP` does not expose an ASGI app, the integration test as written cannot be wired up; the plan doesn't explain how to obtain the ASGI callable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC6 (integration test) / src/rebar/mcp_server.py build_server()

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the private-host rejection mechanism: specify whether it resolves the endpoint hostname to an IP at construction time (using `socket.getaddrinfo` or equivalent) to check against RFC 1918/loopback/link-local ranges, or document the known limitation that only literal IP hostnames are blocked.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that private/link-local/RFC-1918 endpoint hosts 'are rejected by default,' but does not specify the rejection mechanism. A purely lexical check on the URL's hostname string will NOT reject a publicly-routable hostname (e.g., `internal-as.corp.example.com`) that resolves to a private IP — defeating the SSRF protection. The plan leaves the implementation mechanism unspecified, making this an unverified capability assertion.
findings.location: Security section — 'Private/link-local endpoint hosts (RFC 1918, loopback, 169.254.0.0/16) are rejected by default'
findings.suggested_fix: At construction time, resolve the endpoint URL's hostname via `socket.getaddrinfo` and check every returned address with `ipaddress.ip_address(addr).is_private or .is_loopback or .is_link_local`; raise `ValueError` if any result is private (unless `allow_private_host=True`). Note this in the AC for the construction-time guard test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Security section whether the private-host check applies at connection time (post-DNS resolution, e.g. via a custom httpx transport or `socket.getaddrinfo` pre-flight) rather than only at URL-parse/construction time, to prevent DNS-rebinding bypass of the SSRF guard.
findings.finding: The private-host/RFC-1918/loopback/link-local rejection is described as applying to 'endpoint hosts' but the plan does not specify that the check must also apply to the post-DNS-resolution IP address. A construction-time lexical check on the hostname string is bypassable via DNS rebinding or a public hostname that resolves to a private IP, allowing SSRF to internal services.
findings.location: Security section — 'Private/link-local endpoint hosts (RFC 1918, loopback, 169.254.0.0/16) are rejected by default'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'The private-host check MUST be applied to the resolved IP address at connection time (not only the hostname string at construction), e.g. via a custom httpx transport that calls socket.getaddrinfo and rejects RFC-1918/loopback/link-local addresses before the connection is established.'

findings.finding: The construction-time private-host rejection mechanism only works for literal IP addresses in the endpoint URL; a hostname (e.g. `internal-auth.corp.local`) that resolves to an RFC 1918 or loopback address at DNS lookup time will pass the construction guard silently, making the SSRF protection ineffective for the most common real-world internal AS deployment pattern.
findings.location: Security section — 'Private/link-local endpoint hosts … are rejected by default'; AC5 — 'a private/link-local host is rejected … (observable oracle: rejection at construction for the http/private cases)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Security section: 'The construction-time guard checks the literal host component against private/loopback/link-local IP ranges using `ipaddress`; a hostname that resolves to a private IP is **not** caught at construction (documented known gap). Operators relying on `allow_private_host=false` should configure the endpoint as a literal-IP URL or enforce network-level controls.' Update AC5 to explicitly scope the oracle to literal-IP rejection so the implementation is not silently incomplete.

findings.finding: The private-host SSRF guard is specified as 'rejected at construction' and the AC5 oracle is 'rejection at construction for the http/private cases,' but Python's ipaddress module (the natural implementation tool) can only classify literal IP strings — a hostname like `internal-as.corp.local` that resolves to 10.x.x.x at connect time bypasses the construction-time guard silently, violating the stated security invariant that 'private/link-local endpoint hosts are rejected by default.'
findings.location: Security section — 'Private/link-local endpoint hosts (RFC 1918, loopback, 169.254.0.0/16) are rejected by default'; AC5 — 'a private/link-local host is rejected by default … observable oracle: rejection at construction for the http/private cases'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the Security section: 'The construction-time private-host check validates literal IP addresses in the URL; for hostname endpoints, a custom httpx transport (or event hook) that resolves the target IP and checks it against the RFC-1918/loopback/link-local ranges before sending is required to close the SSRF path for the hostname case.' Extend AC5: 'and that a hostname resolving to a private IP is also rejected (or document the limitation explicitly and note reliance on network-layer controls).'

findings.finding: The plan never specifies the fail-closed behavior when the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is absent or empty at runtime. If the verifier proceeds to make an introspection call with no credential (or an empty password), the `client_secret_basic` authentication guarantee on the boundary-crossing path is silently dropped — and if the AS accepts unauthenticated introspection, token validation becomes unauthenticated.
findings.location: Security section — 'client_secret_basic, secret from auth_introspection_client_secret_env'; What section — 'auth_introspection_client_secret_env (NAME of an env var holding the client secret)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section add: 'If the env var named by auth_introspection_client_secret_env is unset or empty at construction time, construction raises ConfigError — fail-closed.' Add an AC5 sub-case: 'and that constructing the verifier when the named env var is absent raises ConfigError (observable oracle: ConfigError at construction, never a verifier instance).'

findings.finding: The Security section mandates that private/link-local endpoint hosts (RFC 1918, loopback, `169.254.0.0/16`) are 'rejected at construction' but never specifies the detection mechanism — whether the check is lexical (URL string / `urllib.parse` hostname extraction + `ipaddress` literal matching) or DNS-resolution-based (resolve the hostname and check the resulting IP). The two approaches have materially different security properties: a lexical check catches `192.168.1.1` but passes `internal-as.corp` (which resolves to a private IP), so a lexical-only implementation is bypassable by using a private hostname instead of a bare IP. Leaving the mechanism implementation-defined means the security control may not achieve its stated goal.
findings.location: Security section — first bullet: 'Private/link-local endpoint hosts … are rejected by default'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'The private-host check parses the literal IP address from the URL (via `ipaddress.ip_address(urllib.parse.urlparse(endpoint).hostname)`); hostnames are NOT DNS-resolved at construction time — an operator using a private hostname must set `allow_private_host=true` (documented risk).' OR: 'The check resolves the hostname at construction (via `socket.getaddrinfo`) and rejects any result that is_private/is_loopback/is_link_local.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before committing AC6's test design, verify the exact FastMCP API for obtaining a Streamable-HTTP ASGI app compatible with httpx.ASGITransport injection (e.g. probe `FastMCP.streamable_http_app()`, `FastMCP.http_app()`, or the mcp>=1.9 HTTP transport constructor surface) and name the specific method/attribute in AC6.
findings.finding: AC6's integration test requires obtaining the Streamable-HTTP ASGI app 'from the configured FastMCP via its Streamable-HTTP entrypoint (resolved at implementation time)'. The current build_server() returns a FastMCP instance used only via .run() (stdio). Multiple probes for the FastMCP Streamable-HTTP ASGI surface (streamable_http_app, http_app, get_asgi_app, StreamableHTTP on mcp.server.fastmcp) all returned UNRESOLVED. The mcp>=1.9 package is installed but the specific API to extract a Starlette/ASGI app from a FastMCP instance for httpx.ASGITransport injection is unverified. If the entrypoint method has a different name or signature than assumed, the integration test cannot be written as specified without rework.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6 (integration test)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete FastMCP method/attribute that exposes the Streamable-HTTP ASGI app in AC6 (e.g. `server.streamable_http_app()` or the relevant transport kwarg), replacing 'resolved at implementation time' with the chosen API call.
findings.finding: AC6 (integration test) defers a real design decision to the executor: it says the ASGI app should be obtained 'from the configured FastMCP via its Streamable-HTTP entrypoint (resolved at implementation time)' and explicitly forbids using `build_server()`'s return — but names no concrete API, method, or attribute to call. An executor cannot act on this without stopping to investigate which FastMCP public surface exposes the Streamable-HTTP ASGI app.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6 (integration test)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(resolved at implementation time)' with the specific call, for example: 'obtained by calling `fastmcp_instance.streamable_http_app()` (or equivalent public entrypoint — look up in FastMCP docs/source and pin the method name here before implementation begins).'  Add a one-line note explaining why `build_server()`'s return is insufficient (e.g. it wraps additional middleware that changes the auth path).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or spec clause that defines and tests the behavior when `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is unset or empty (e.g., construction raises `ConfigurationError`), and add a corresponding unit test asserting the fail-fast behavior.
findings.finding: No AC covers the failure path when `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` names a non-existent or empty environment variable — it is unspecified whether this is a construction-time error or a silent runtime failure, leaving the security-critical credential path untested for misconfiguration.
findings.location: Security section / AC5
findings.suggested_fix: In AC5, add: 'Construction raises ConfigurationError (or equivalent) when the env var named by auth_introspection_client_secret_env is absent or empty; a unit test asserts this.'

findings.finding: The plan does not specify the behavior when the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is absent or empty at construction time, and no AC tests this guard — yet the security requirement ('no plaintext secret in config') implies a hard failure is required.
findings.location: What section ('auth_introspection_client_secret_env (NAME of an env var holding the client secret — no plaintext secret in config)'); AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC5: 'and that constructing the verifier when the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is unset raises `ConfigurationError` at construction time (observable oracle: `pytest.raises(ConfigurationError)`).'; add one sentence to the Security section: 'If the env var is absent or empty at construction time, raise `ConfigurationError` immediately — never defer to call time.'

findings.finding: The fallback behavior when `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` names an environment variable that is absent or empty at runtime is entirely unspecified. The plan defines the config key as holding the NAME of an env var (not the secret itself), but provides no decision rule for the secret-resolution failure path: should construction of `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` raise immediately? Should it raise at first call? What exception type? This is a structural gap in the sub-step failure protocol.
findings.location: What section — '`auth_introspection_client_secret_env` (NAME of an env var holding the client secret — no plaintext secret in config)'; Security section — 'secret sourced from `auth_introspection_client_secret_env`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'If the environment variable named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is absent or empty at construction time, raise `ConfigError` immediately (fail-closed, fail-fast — never send an empty credential).' Add a corresponding AC5 sub-bullet: 'construction raises when the named env var is missing.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the `env-vars.md` generator (`gen_env_registry.py`) will pick up the new `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_INTROSPECTION_*` vars — confirm they are read via a recognized literal `os.environ` pattern, or add a new `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS` entry, rather than relying on a claimed (but unverified) MCP_ENV_VARS-iteration fix.
findings.finding: The plan claims `env-vars.md` is 'regenerated for the full key set by S7; S1 fixes the generators to iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS`'. However, `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` generates `env-vars.md` by AST-scanning `os.environ` reads in `src/rebar` — it does NOT iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS`. The new `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_INTROSPECTION_*` env vars will only appear in `env-vars.md` if they are accessed via a recognized literal `os.environ` pattern in the new source file. If the implementation reads them via a helper or indirection, the CI drift gate will pass but the env-var registry will silently omit them.
findings.location: What section: 'these auto-derive the `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_INTROSPECTION_*` **env-var names**, which are added to `MCP_ENV_VARS` … and `server.json`; … The generated `mcp-reference.md`/`env-vars.md` are regenerated for the full key set by S7; S1 fixes the generators to iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS`'
findings.suggested_fix: Ensure `_mcp_auth.py` reads the new env vars via a literal `os.environ.get("REBAR_MCP_AUTH_INTROSPECTION_...")` call (directly, not through a non-enumerated helper), so `gen_env_registry.py`'s AST scanner picks them up automatically without requiring changes to the generator.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC6's proving command by naming the specific ASGI entrypoint and adding an assertion that the verifier is invoked (e.g. the stateful fake's request count increments once per end-to-end request), so that the test cannot pass if the auth middleware is absent from the chosen ASGI app.
findings.finding: The proving command for AC6's integration test cannot be fully verified because the plan does not specify which FastMCP ASGI entrypoint is used. If the executor picks an entrypoint that bypasses the authentication middleware, `make test` will be green while the integration criterion ('introspection-validated token → 200 end-to-end') is not actually exercised on the correct code path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6 (integration test)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC6: 'confirm the verifier is on the critical path by asserting the introspection fake\'s hit-count increments for each authenticated request; the test must fail if the verifier is removed from the middleware chain.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC assertion covering the `client_id` mapping: (a) present in response → used directly; (b) absent but `sub` present → falls back to `sub`; (c) both absent → `"unknown"`.
findings.finding: The `client_id` field mapping — including the fallback logic ('absent → falls back to sub, else "unknown"') — is described in the What section but is not covered by any AC bullet. No observable oracle exists to verify this behavior.
findings.location: What section (client_id mapping) vs Acceptance Criteria (all bullets)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC bullet 2 or add a new bullet: 'Unit test asserts client_id field mapping: when the introspection response includes client_id it is used directly; when client_id is absent but sub is present, client_id falls back to sub; when both are absent, client_id is "unknown".'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to AC bullet 2 (or a new assertion) an observable oracle for the `sub`→`subject` mapping: present in response → `subject` equals the value; absent → `subject is None`.
findings.finding: The `sub`→`subject` mapping (absent → `None`) is described in the What section but no AC bullet contains an observable oracle asserting this field's value or its None fallback.
findings.location: What section (sub→subject mapping) vs Acceptance Criteria (all bullets)
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC bullet 2 to include: 'and that a response with sub set yields AccessToken.subject equal to that value, while a response omitting sub yields subject=None.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CLAUDE.md / ADR entry capturing the decision to write a custom IntrospectionTokenVerifier (rather than using the SDK example class) and enumerating the production hardening it adds, so future contributors have a durable canonical reference.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a deliberate architectural decision — writing a custom IntrospectionTokenVerifier rather than importing the SDK's class, with bespoke security hardening (private-host rejection, fail-closed transport, custom audience validation) — but does not call for capturing this decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc update; future maintainers may not understand why the SDK class was bypassed or may inadvertently revert to it.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section; NOTE in 'What' section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to CLAUDE.md (or a new docs/adr/0NNN-introspection-verifier.md) stating: 'We do not import the SDK's IntrospectionTokenVerifier because it is explicitly documented as an example, not a hardened class. Our implementation adds: client_secret_basic authentication, HTTPS-only enforcement, private-host rejection, fail-closed transport errors, and strict audience validation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `auth_introspection_connect_timeout` / `auth_introspection_read_timeout` config keys (with the stated defaults) to the `auth_introspection_*` family, or explicitly document in the plan that timeouts are intentionally fixed constants (not operator-tunable) in v1 with a follow-on story to add them.
findings.finding: The HTTP timeout defaults (connect 5s / read 10s) are hardcoded constants with no config key, meaning operators whose Authorization Server is slow (e.g. on a high-latency internal network) cannot tune them without a code change.
findings.location: Security section — 'a bounded HTTP timeout (default connect 5s / read 10s)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a retry/backoff policy (e.g., 1–2 retries with short exponential backoff on transient errors: connection errors, 429, 503) to the introspection call, or explicitly justify why even transient errors must deny immediately (e.g., document the reliability vs. security tradeoff and accept it as a known limitation).
findings.finding: The plan specifies fail-closed on ANY error (non-200, timeout, malformed) with no retry or backoff for transient network errors. A momentary AS blip (brief DNS hiccup, single dropped packet) will deny every authenticated request for its duration, with no opportunity to recover. There is no circuit-breaker, retry-with-backoff, or degraded-mode fallback mentioned.
findings.location: Security section — 'Fail-closed transport: a non-200, timeout, or malformed response raises'; Acceptance Criteria — AC for non-200/timeout behavior
findings.suggested_fix: Add one retry with a short fixed delay (e.g., 500ms) for connection-level errors and 429/503 HTTP responses. Network errors and 5xx from the AS are transient; 400/401 from the AS are definitive rejections and should not be retried. Document this explicitly in the Security section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: introspection failures (non-200, timeout, malformed response) must emit a structured log event at WARN/ERROR level (including endpoint URL, HTTP status, error type — but never the token or secret) so operators can diagnose AS connectivity issues.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability instrumentation for introspection failures. When the AS is unreachable or returns errors, operators have no metric, log line, or alert to diagnose why authenticated requests are being denied. The only constraint is 'Tokens/secrets never logged', which does not preclude logging failure events.
findings.location: Security section — 'Tokens/secrets never logged'; no instrumentation requirement anywhere in the plan or ACs
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section or a new Observability section: 'On introspection failure, log at WARN/ERROR: endpoint URL, HTTP status code (if available), error category (timeout / non-200 / malformed), and request ID — never the token value or client secret.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC assertion (or extend AC2/AC3) that verifies the `client_id` field mapping: (a) response includes `client_id` → AccessToken.client_id equals it; (b) response omits `client_id` but includes `sub` → AccessToken.client_id equals sub; (c) both absent → AccessToken.client_id equals `"unknown"`.
findings.finding: The `client_id` field mapping defined in the spec ('absent → falls back to sub, else "unknown"') has no corresponding AC assertion; if the fallback logic is wrong, no test catches it.
findings.location: What section (client_id mapping) vs Acceptance Criteria (AC2, AC3)
findings.suggested_fix: In AC2 or AC3, add: 'Unit test asserts AccessToken.client_id field mapping: present → used directly; absent with sub present → falls back to sub; both absent → "unknown".'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the What/Approach section an explicit statement that `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` accepts an optional injectable `httpx.Client` (or `AsyncClient`) parameter to enable the stateful-fake injection described in the integration AC.
findings.finding: The integration test AC relies on injecting a custom httpx client into the verifier ('injected via the verifier's httpx client so NO real network call is made'), but neither the What section nor the Approach section specifies that the IntrospectionTokenVerifier must expose an injectable httpx client interface. This creates a cross-section gap: the AC assumes a testability seam the implementation description does not guarantee.
findings.location: Integration test AC bullet (last AC) vs What section / Approach section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'The verifier accepts an optional httpx.Client (or AsyncClient) constructor parameter (default: a new client with the configured timeout); when supplied, it is used instead of the default — enabling test injection of the stateful fake without patching.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that S2 is merged (or explicitly scope S4 to also add the `auth_strategies` config key to `McpConfig` and `_SECTIONS['mcp']`) before assuming the activation seam exists.
findings.finding: The plan's S2 dependency seam — the `auth_strategies` config key said to have been 'introduced by S2' — does not exist anywhere in the codebase. `McpConfig` has only `readonly`, `allow_llm`, and `allow_jira_sync`; `_SECTIONS['mcp']` has exactly those three keys; no `auth_strategies` appears anywhere under `src/rebar`. S4's activation mechanism ("including `introspection` in the `mcp.auth_strategies` list") rests entirely on this absent deliverable.
findings.location: What section: 'activated by including `introspection` in the `mcp.auth_strategies` list (the strategy-selection config key **introduced by S2**)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a concrete prerequisite check: either confirm S2's `auth_strategies` field is present in `McpConfig` before starting S4, or explicitly list 'add `mcp.auth_strategies` to `McpConfig`/`_SECTIONS["mcp"]`' as a S4 task if S2 is not yet merged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm S2 is merged and `auth_resource_server_url` is present in `McpConfig`/`_SECTIONS['mcp']` before S4 begins, or explicitly scope S4 to add it.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `auth_resource_server_url` was 'introduced by S2' and that S4 uses it to stamp `resource=auth_resource_server_url` on the `AccessToken`. A full codebase search finds zero occurrences of this identifier anywhere under `src/rebar`. The field does not exist.
findings.location: What section: 'validates against the single `auth_resource_server_url` (S2)' and 'stamps `resource=auth_resource_server_url`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py` is created from scratch by S4 (or S2), and specify what S2 must have placed there before S4 begins.
findings.finding: The plan's entire file-placement claim — 'Add an `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` to `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py`' — asserts a file that does not yet exist. Combined with the absent S2 seam (no `auth_strategies`, no `auth_resource_server_url`), it is unverifiable whether S2 established any scaffolding in this file or whether S4 is expected to create it from scratch. The plan does not say 'create' vs. 'add to existing'.
findings.location: What section: 'Add an **`IntrospectionTokenVerifier`** to `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the introspection httpx client is constructed with `follow_redirects=False` (or equivalent) so the Basic credential is never forwarded to a redirect target; add a construction-time test asserting no redirect is followed.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify that HTTP redirects must be disabled on the introspection httpx client. By default httpx follows 3xx redirects and re-sends persistent headers (including Authorization: Basic) to the redirect target — a misconfigured or compromised AS that returns a 302 pointing at an attacker-controlled HTTPS host would exfiltrate the client_secret to that host.
findings.location: Security section — 'Call the introspection endpoint over authenticated TLS (`client_secret_basic`, secret from `auth_introspection_client_secret_env`)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section, add: 'The introspection httpx client MUST be constructed with `follow_redirects=False`; a 3xx response is treated the same as a non-200 (raises → deny).' Add a corresponding AC assertion that a 302 response raises rather than follows.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define (or inline from S2) the full `mcp.auth_strategies` enum vocabulary — all valid member values, their mutual coexistence rules, and the composite-dispatch decision protocol — so S4 can be implemented and reviewed without requiring S2 to be delivered first.
findings.finding: The plan activates the verifier by including `"introspection"` in `mcp.auth_strategies`, describing it as 'the strategy-selection config key introduced by S2'. The full enum vocabulary of `auth_strategies` — all valid member values, what happens when multiple strategies coexist, and the composite-dispatch protocol — is never defined anywhere in this plan and is attributed entirely to S2, which does not exist in the codebase (confirmed: zero matches for `auth_strategies` in src/rebar). An implementor writing the `McpConfig` field and the dispatch logic has no specification for the complete value set.
findings.location: What section — 'activated by including `introspection` in the `mcp.auth_strategies` list (the strategy-selection config key **introduced by S2**, this story's dependency)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Config seam contract' subsection that lists: the complete set of valid `auth_strategies` values (e.g. `['introspection', 'jwt', ...]`), the coercer type (`list[str]` vs `set[str]`), and the dispatch rule when multiple strategies are present (first-match? any-accepts? all-must-accept?). If this is truly S2's domain, cite the exact S2 AC or section that defines it and note S4 is blocked until that AC is verifiable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the composite-check decision rule for the `resource` field: specify (inline or by precise reference to S2's AC) what value `auth_resource_server_url` has at runtime, how the composite verifier reads `AccessToken.resource`, and what comparison makes the composite check pass/fail.
findings.finding: The plan describes the 'composite check passes' outcome — stamping `resource=auth_resource_server_url` on the AccessToken so a composite verifier accepts it — but the composite-check decision protocol (how the outer composite verifier uses the `resource` field, what it compares it against, pass/fail semantics) is defined entirely in S2 and is never co-located with the `AccessToken` mapping schema described here. S2 does not exist in the codebase.
findings.location: What section — 'stamps `resource=auth_resource_server_url` so the composite check passes'

findings.finding: The Security section requires a 'bounded timeout' on the introspection HTTP call but states no concrete value or range; AC4 tests that a timeout causes a raise but never specifies the timeout duration to configure or assert, making the 'bounded' property unmeasurable and leaving an executor free to pick an arbitrary value.
findings.location: Security section ('bounded timeout'); AC4 ('non-200 / timeout / malformed response raises')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section replace 'bounded timeout' with 'a 10-second total timeout (connect + read)'; add a constructor parameter `timeout_seconds: float = 10.0` to `McpConfig` or the verifier constructor, and extend AC5 with: 'and that the httpx client is constructed with `timeout=10.0` (observable oracle: `client.timeout.read == 10.0`)'.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'reuse the already-present httpx' to justify using httpx directly in `_mcp_auth.py`, but httpx is NOT a declared dependency of the `[mcp]` extra (`pyproject.toml` line 82: `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9"]`). The codebase's optionality CI (`test_core_optionality.py`) explicitly classifies `httpx` as a heavy dep that arrives only via `[agents]`/pydantic-ai and must stay lazy everywhere. A user who installs `nava-rebar[mcp]` without `[agents]` will get an `ImportError` at runtime when the introspection verifier imports httpx.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — 'Reuse the already-present httpx'; pyproject.toml [project.optional-dependencies] mcp
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"httpx>=0.27"` to the `mcp` extra in pyproject.toml: `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9", "httpx>=0.27"]`. Alternatively, if mcp>=1.9 already requires httpx as a transitive dep, add a comment in pyproject.toml and the plan naming that transitive chain explicitly so it cannot silently break on an mcp version bump.

findings.finding: The plan says 'bounded timeout' but never specifies the concrete timeout value, unit, or an AC done-definition. An implementer could set 30 s and still satisfy the spec, blocking the hot path for every token validation.
findings.location: Security section — 'bounded timeout'; AC has no corresponding timeout oracle
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Security: 'Default timeout: 5 s (configurable via auth_introspection_timeout_ms); raises TimeoutError after that bound.' Add an AC: 'Unit test asserts the verifier raises within the configured timeout when the fake AS stalls.'

findings.finding: The plan states 'bounded timeout' for the introspection HTTP call but never specifies the timeout value, its default, or whether it is a configurable key — so operators cannot tune it and no AC verifies it.
findings.location: Security section ('bounded timeout'); Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers timeout value or config)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `auth_introspection_timeout` (int, default 5) to the config key list alongside the other `auth_introspection_*` keys, and add an AC line: 'Unit test asserts the httpx introspection request is constructed with a timeout equal to `auth_introspection_timeout`; a fake that never responds triggers the timeout and raises within that bound.'

findings.finding: The plan specifies a 'bounded timeout' for the introspection HTTP call but declares no timeout value, no configurable field, and no default. An unspecified or excessively long timeout allows a slow or adversarial Authorization Server to hold open httpx connections indefinitely, creating a resource-exhaustion vector on the boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: Security section — 'bounded timeout'; Approach & rationale — 'Reuse the already-present httpx'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hard-coded default (e.g., `timeout=5.0`) to the httpx POST call, document it in the Security section, and extend AC3's 'non-200 / timeout / malformed response raises' oracle to include a slow-response scenario. Optionally expose it as `auth_introspection_timeout` (seconds) in the config list.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
